column5: Question
I enjoy trying out new types of diets or eating styles, even if they're unfamiliar.
I seek out unusual or exotic ingredients when I cook or eat out.
I’m curious about how food affects not just my body but also my mood and mind.
I like to experiment with new recipes rather than sticking to familiar meals.
I am open to switching my diet based on new research or trends.
I actively read or watch content about innovative or holistic nutrition approaches.
I’m comfortable changing my eating habits if I believe it might benefit my well-being.
I enjoy trying out new forms of physical activity, even if I’m not good at them yet.
I prefer workout routines that include variety and creativity over repetition.
I am open to exploring unconventional fitness methods like dance, martial arts, or acrobatics.
I enjoy setting fitness goals that challenge me to try something new.
I’m curious about how different types of training (e.g. HIIT, yoga, calisthenics) affect my body.
I enjoy learning the science behind physical training and body performance.
I like switching up my workout environment — outdoors, gym, home, etc.
I’ve experimented with different sleep routines to see what works best for me.
I am willing to adjust my rest habits based on new insights about recovery and performance.
I explore tools like wearables or sleep-tracking apps to improve my rest quality.
I’m open to practices like cold exposure, massage, or stretching for recovery.
I consider rest as an evolving part of wellness, not just “doing nothing.”
I’ve tried or am open to trying non-traditional recovery practices (e.g., float therapy, biohacking sleep).
I’m interested in mindfulness, journaling, or other self-awareness practices.
I enjoy reading or listening to content that challenges my thinking about health or life.
I see wellness as not just physical, but also emotional and mental evolution.
I’m open to coaching, therapy, or guided introspection to improve my mindset.
I reflect on how my beliefs and emotions affect my health behaviors.
I like exploring unfamiliar wellness philosophies, even if they’re outside the mainstream.
I believe growth in wellness comes from trying new ideas, even at the risk of failure.
I track what I eat to ensure it aligns with my wellness goals.
I stick to a consistent eating schedule most days.
I plan meals in advance rather than making spontaneous food choices.
I avoid junk food or indulgences even when tempted.
I follow my nutrition commitments even when I’m busy or stressed.
I follow a consistent workout routine without skipping sessions.
I set specific fitness goals and track my progress over time.
I prepare my workout gear or schedule in advance.
I rarely let mood or weather interfere with my exercise plans.
I enjoy pushing through challenges to complete my workouts.
I maintain a consistent sleep schedule, even on weekends.
I avoid screen time, food, or distractions before bed to protect my sleep quality.
I schedule rest and recovery days purposefully to avoid burnout.
I’m disciplined about winding down mentally before sleep.
I prioritize quality rest, even when life gets hectic.
I regularly reflect on whether my habits are aligned with my goals.
I journal, track, or review my mental and emotional well-being.
I’m methodical in how I work on my mindset or mental health.
I follow through on wellness strategies even when motivation is low.
I avoid impulsive actions that could derail my progress.
I enjoy discussing nutrition or wellness with others.
I’m more motivated to eat healthy when I’m part of a group or challenge.
I like sharing meals with others who care about wellness.
I enjoy being influenced by others’ healthy habits.
I find energy and joy in shared food experiences (e.g., meal prep groups, social dining).
I prefer group workouts, classes, or team sports over solo exercise.
I’m more consistent with my workouts when I have accountability partners.
I feel energized by high-intensity or dynamic fitness environments.
I like participating in fitness challenges or competitions.
I get bored easily when exercising alone or in silence.
I struggle to wind down when I’m alone for too long.
I find it easier to relax when others are doing the same with me.
I feel energized by active social recovery (e.g., walks, spa days with friends).
I prefer guided recovery sessions or group meditations over doing it solo.
I’m more motivated to sleep well when someone holds me accountable.
I open up about my emotions more easily when I’m with others.
I enjoy group discussions or podcasts about mindset and wellness.
I thrive in wellness environments where people share and connect.
I feel most mentally refreshed after social interaction.
I often express my feelings out loud as a way to process them.
I encourage others to eat healthier or share wellness tips kindly.
I adapt my eating habits to avoid conflict or accommodate others.
I prefer harmony during shared meals and avoid pushing my preferences.
I enjoy preparing or sharing healthy meals with others.
I try to avoid judgment when people choose differently from me nutritionally.
I feel motivated when others join me in physical activity.
I offer support or encouragement to friends in their wellness efforts.
I prefer collaborative or non-competitive forms of exercise.
I’m patient with others who are starting their fitness journey.
I try to be a positive influence in shared workout settings.
I respect others’ rest time and expect the same in return.
I value peaceful environments for winding down and recovery.
I avoid disturbing others when they’re resting or recharging.
I feel guilty if I prioritize my rest over others’ needs.
I tend to adjust my rest routine to match that of close friends or family.
I try to create emotional space for others to talk or heal.
I often prioritize others’ emotional needs over my own.
I am affected emotionally when people close to me are unwell.
I tend to downplay my stress to avoid burdening others.
I prefer collaborative approaches to emotional wellness (e.g., peer support, counseling).
I tend to overeat or undereat when I feel emotionally stressed.
I often feel guilty after indulging in food.
I second-guess my food choices even when I’ve planned them.
I feel anxious when I deviate from a diet or nutrition plan.
I frequently seek emotional comfort through food.
I get discouraged if I miss a workout or don’t perform well.
I sometimes feel self-conscious about how I look when exercising.
I avoid certain exercises for fear of doing them wrong or being judged.
I’m often too anxious or drained to begin working out, even if I want to.
I worry more about failure than progress in fitness.
I have trouble falling asleep because my mind races with thoughts.
I feel guilty when I take time to rest or do nothing.
I frequently feel unrested even after sleep.
I worry that I’m not doing enough to “optimize” my recovery.
I feel uneasy when my routine is disrupted, especially before bed.
I often feel overwhelmed by stress or emotion.
I experience strong emotional highs and lows in short periods.
I worry a lot about my health and wellness progress.
I struggle to stay mentally present when I feel off-track.
I ruminate over past failures or missed goals related to wellness.
